speaker: interviewer
text: 19th, 1997. Doctor Phibbs, we left off on the last tape-- I had asked you how it was for you being in battle, and you cited an experience with seeing the first soldier die. Is there anything else you'd like to say about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember one of our officers on our staff came into the aid station one time, during the big battle at Herrlisheim, the Bulge battle for us, and seeing-- listening to the screaming, the crying, and the wounded. And he was shaking, and he said, Doc, an aid station is a hell of a place to fight a war, isn't it? It's a hell of a place. They suddenly saw the reality of it, which they didn't see any other time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's something you can't describe, I guess. You have to be in a war to know what a war is like. It's-- you're outside all human dimensions. You're in a world where nothing you ever learned obtains anymore.

words.text: And he was shaking, and he said,

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the position, then, where you needed to triage patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, fast. And brutally, sometimes. There were one or two doctors for 20 or 30 badly-wounded men. So we'd work fast. And our aid men got to be pretty experienced. If someone was going to die, we would make them comfortable. We'd concentrate on the people we could save. And triage is what we did all the time, you bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your unit ever take any prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many. Started taking prisoners as soon as we got on the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treated well. They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never saw any brutality or ill treatment. If they get-- when they were first captured up front, if they were giving somebody a lot of sass or lip, as we would've said in my generation, they might be kicked in the rear end to hurry them along with the other prisoners. That's about as far as it ever went. No, they were treated-- Geneva Conference, articles of war-- very well indeed. And we took a lot of prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anything ever confiscated from them that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: From prisoners? Gee, I didn't, that I can remember. I don't think that-- I don't think people did. We had a prisoner-of-war interrogation team right with our combat command headquarters. And this was a refugee from Germany, an anti-Nazi German. And he loved interrogating those Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would threaten them, but they'd never harm them. Ever. They used psychological threatening and all kinds of gestures with guns and things to make them talk, but nobody was ever hurt. Did they use you for your German abilities, language abilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a whole team there, but I would just spontaneously pick up-- the prisoners were so happy to be taken alive, and they'd heard so much propaganda about the Jewish-American, Roosevelt, Bolshevik murderers who were going to torture them and so on. And they found we were nice guys, we gave them-- [LAUGHS] bandaged up their wounds, gave them a shot, maybe something to eat. That they'd talk. I couldn't shut them up. Who their regiment was, where they were coming from, which officer they didn't like, who was a big Nazi, who wasn't. Yadda, yadda. And they-- they found I was not going to hurt them, I was helping them, and I spoke German. I couldn't keep them quiet. [LAUGHS] There was no trouble getting information out of the wounded German prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you cared for the wounded German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, exactly like ours. I took care of hundreds and hundreds of wounded Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact at all with any of the civilian German population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was after the breakthrough started, yeah. I left off with the Battle of the Bulge, our end of it, the Alsatian Bulge. We went-- then we went down. We liberated the city of Colmar. They stopped the Bulge fighting with trying to prepare for our big offensive that ended the war. In February, we were loaned to the French First Army. And we-- three American divisions went south of the great city of Colmar, and we liberated it. The whole southern Alsace, where-- the Swiss border. And we had the nice experience of rolling into a liberated city with people screaming and yelling and waving and handing flowers and bottles of wine through the windows, and so on. And it was a savage fight for a while, because again, it was Heinrich Himmler-- prestige area, with his SS troops. They were fighting the 10th SS Mountain Division. And we met the French-- Algerian division coming up from the Swiss border, and that was it. The last French soil was cleared.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I had an interesting experience. I wrote it in my book. Because I speak German, I was involved in picking up the leading Nazi collaborator in lower Alsace. Do you have time to talk about it? Absolutely. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Ulmar, as I recall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- after the fighting stopped, we set up our command post in a Gasthaus, an inn. And we looked around for the owner-- usually, you made a deal for the occupation forces, you'll be paid for your this, the yadda-- is it all right? Because this is France, after all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody around. And about evening, a little Alsatian peasant came up. He was talking to the guard and the intelligence officer, who didn't speak a word of either French or German. So I went out and talked to him, and he said, do you want to pick up the biggest Nazi collaborator in all Alsace? And I said, certainly we do.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, he's the Burgermeister of this town. That's his inn you're in that you took over. So we all picked up Tommy guns and got two Jeeps, and we got the little farm-- the little farmer was so afraid of this man, he didn't want to go with us. Little man with a cap and a half-inch cigarette sticking out of his face. We got them in the Jeeps, and we wound down through the streets. Then we walked up to the door, and in my best German cop method-- which I got from reading German detective stories-- i knocked at the door, and I said-- this-- we found out-- they told us, I should say-- he's hiding in the house of a Jew that he sent to a concentration camp. Not in his own house.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this guy knew where he was. So I knocked on the door, and a white-haired woman appeared. And I said, Frau Ulmar? Ja. I said, Herr Ulmar, is he-- Yes, in said German. I said, Bitte kommen sie mit [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess-- I'm forgetting-- he came to the door. Now, he looked like Emil Jennings in a 1920-some movie. He had [INAUDIBLE], you know, oppression-style, cropped gray hair, kind of heavy, with a watch chain, six chins. And he said, Yo-- It all comes back to me-- he said, Yo. Alsace for ja.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Herr Ulmar? Yo. And I said, [GERMAN]. Please come with us. We'd like to place a few questions. That was the standard thing the SS, the [LAUGHS] Gestapo used. He knew exactly-- everyone in [INAUDIBLE]-- he had a wife and two grown daughters, and they all started screaming, and yelling, and waving, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked in. I remember, I was carrying a Tommy gun. And-- about, it was too cold. We couldn't take their poor, old father out in the cold, and he'd catch pneumonia. And [LAUGHS] I said, no, we're going to bundle him up very warmly. Yadda. This was quite a painful scene. Finally he got a fedora hat and a scarf, and we took him off. And I remember-- as-- you know, I'm an American, after all. I'm not used to be a police-- a Gestapo type. And I was feeling sorry for the family. So we got him down into the Jeep, and there's some soldiers down there, working on some wine they'd liberated from our infantry battalion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them said, who's the old guy, Doc? And I said, he's the biggest Nazi collaborator around here. We're going to turn him over to the French. The daughter turned to him, the girl who spoke pretty good English. She said, please, please. The soldiers said, oh, lady, don't worry. All they're going to do is shoot the old son of a bitch. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, oh, God, get us of here. So we got in the Jeep and took off, and held him overnight. And we got a radio from the French First Army when they heard we had him, and they sent a young officer down in an old French Army blue overcoat. Next day, they pick him up. And our colonel said, now tell us what he did. Well, he sent all the-- rounded up the Jews of the town, sent them to concentration camps to be killed. He did this, and he tortured the resistance people. He was an out and out Nazi. And he said, the last thing he did, he got hostages. Just picked up-- rounded up innocent people as hostages for something the underground had done, set them out in the Valley of the Vosges and let them freeze to death slowly under German machine guns. And he said that was the last thing this monster did. And I remember that as we drove into Colmar, one of the first things we saw-- the French were with us, the French First Army-- there was a big line of Senegalese riflemen-- eight or 10 or 11 of them-- facing a wall. So then we realized it was a firing squad. They were shooting collaborators. The French didn't give them-- didn't take very long to administer justice. And in the course of taking care of the wounded in the town, a sister-- a nun came to our aid station. [INAUDIBLE] So I said, fine, I'll take care of the wounded. We drove around with this nun, Sister Rose. And this one little old grandmother had a compound fracture of the femur. The bones were sticking out through the skin. She'd been there for two days. [INAUDIBLE] We splinted it, put traction-- did the things you do. We hauled her down in a litter down these winding stairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she got off some line about, I better not talk to the handsome major anymore. You're going to drop me or something. She sounds so funny. I said, Gee, you remind of some Jewish grandmothers I knew in the Bronx. She got terrified. She said, how'd you know? [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- she said, ja. She was Jewish, all right. She was the only one left. She said, ich bin allein. [GERMAN] Everyone else is dead, dead, dead. I'm the only-- [GERMAN]. She said, an old witch like me. I'm still alive. So I said, Grandma, [GERMAN]. Jewish is good. Jewish is heroic. Jewish gets you a medal. [LAUGHS] And we took her into the big city hospital at Colmar, about 10 miles away, and delivered her over to the French doctors, and got her taken care of. She said, don't tell them. Don't tell them. She was still so afraid, you know, because the Vichy French were as antisemitic as the Germans. She kept saying, [GERMAN]. I remember the young French doctor-- they're all bilingual, Alsatians-- said, gee, you speak good German. I said, yeah, learned it in school. And he said, you two got a big secret. You know, big secret. We'll never-- she didn't want them to know she was Jewish, even after--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she manage to conceal her identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Well, from them anyway. And she'd hidden out in this house. She was the last Jew in Herrlisheim [INAUDIBLE] Colmar. The others were all killed. Little old lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go from Colmar? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to reserve. Pick up tanks, replace infantry, casualties, of course. And we-- we were in reserve for a couple of weeks. And then we went on a night march up through Luxembourg to Third Army. Third Army was Patton, the much-advertised Patton's army on our left flank. We were the veterans, and Seventh Army. But what happened was the Sauer-Moselle Triangle flows into the Rhine. The Rhine makes kind of a loop, a curve toward the west. It starts in Switzerland, flows north, then turns northwest. And the Moselle flows into it, and so there's a big trapezoid formed from the south. That's called the Sauer Basin-- a big industrial area. And the Germans had their Siegfried Line forts all along the front of it, which we'd been battering against. But the Third Army on our left had broken through the Siegfried Line, was all way up the Moselle to the Rhine. So the flank was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they loaned our division to Third Army. We had a long night march through Luxembourg, up the Moselle, through the burning city of Trier, and over the pontoon bridge and into Germany. And then we were in a wild, cavalry, shoot-them-up campaign that lasted about 10 days. In the rear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three Army divisions abreast, we slammed into the rear of a whole German Army Group, who were facing south. And they looked over their shoulders and here we came. So it was mad. At One point, we didn't sleep for three days and three nights, once, I remember. You'd be running through German artillery from the rear with horses galloping through the flames. And we finally met the Seventh Army coming up at the city of Speyer on the Rhine. We were the lead division in the whole Third Army attack in the Sauer Basin. We led the way to the Rhine. And because we weren't Third Army, Georgie Patton wouldn't release our division identity. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the newspapers knew perfectly well who we were, the correspondents, the called us the mystery division. Mystery division leads Patton's army to the Rhine, that was us. And then we rested there a little bit, and went back toward Seventh Army, crossed under smokescreen. In the dead of night, crossed the Rhine at Mannheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And attacked straight east into the Odenwald, the mountains that are a continuation north of the Schwarzwald-- the Black Forest. And then we really went-- the US Army official histories describe the 12th Army. They said, they went through the tangles of the Odenwald like a bobcat through-- like a scalded cat through a thicket. We really moved in little task forces. That's when I had a lot to do with Germans. I met them everywhere. I talked to them everywhere, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your experiences with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first was the-- the nonsense-- all the walls, all the defiance we saw. You know, Nazi slogans and graffiti on all the walls. But then they all gave up. We call it being snow blind. As we roll into a town, it'd be fluttering with bed sheets, pillowcases, handkerchiefs, anything white they could get out would be fluttering out the window. They-- they gave up fast. The first thing we learned. The second thing we learned is-- of course, we were advance. We were-- we'd go through fast. The Infantry might take a day or two to catch up with us, so there's a gap in there. And what the Germans themselves were terrified of was their own SS. Because they'd come out of the woods and kill any Germans that hung out a white flag, if the Americans had gone on ahead, or they'd left a little vacuum in the rear. The Germans would say to me, [GERMAN]. The SS, they're not human anymore. They were terrified of themselves that they-- the SS were criminals, no question about it. And the German civilians were as afraid of as anybody else was, I suppose. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember in the city of Speyer on the Rhine, helping-- there were a crowd of German civilians huddled across the street. We had an aid station in a Gasthaus, an inn, and we're running ambulances up and down. It was very heavy fighting. There was one young German soldier had a leg badly torn up, and there were steps. So I took his arm, got him over my shoulder, and helped-- carried him down the steps. And all the Germans were astounded. They go, look, look. He's helping a German, he's helping a German. They couldn't believe that we took such good care of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then we discovered that-- several things. How two-faced they were. We-- we'd go through these towns, and we-- I'm-- I had an old cross-country ambulance right in the middle of tanks, and I'd be the first Red Cross. And so, we-- our prisoners, who had been captured by the Germans and marched hard, starved, 25 miles a day-- we'd pick them up, liberate them ourselves. And I had the interesting experience of going through a little town, and all the Germans are waving and acting like the-- Lederhosen, Bavarian [INAUDIBLE], how happy we are. You know, waving white flags. And the prisoner was sitting with me in the ambulance, said, ha. They marched us through here a few days ago. He said, let's get out and I'll show you some guys. He said, I recognize these people. He said, that guy threw me a rock in my face. That guy hit us with a bucket of horse manure. That guy hit us with a shovel. They were screaming at-- you know, [GERMAN], curse the Jews, Heil Hitler, all the rest of it. And then we came and they were 180 degrees. So I remember I terrified this particular bunch, I got out a-- a medical tag, an emergency medical tag that we put on the wounded. It looked like something official.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went along saying, wie heisst du? What's your name? [GERMAN]. And I pretended I was taking down names and addresses. They were all terrified. They thought the American had an SS too. [LAUGHS] But the joke among the Allied forces were the Nazis all evaporated as soon as we crossed the Rhine. Me, a Nazi? Never. I've never-- No. What made you think? I-- no, no, no. These are the few fanatics. All us Germans are just kindly, nice people. And the most hypocritical bunch of people I've ever seen in my life. They swore they'd never had anything to do with the Nazis or-- and, you know, Germany was 99% Nazi. During this time, did you have any correspondence with anybody from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wrote home to my wife all the time. My father, my kid brother. We got emails, sure, back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you able to write about everything that was going on, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty much. They didn't-- I knew what not to write about. We were supposed to have our mail censored. But censoring mail for a couple million people is an almost impossible job. Officers' mail wasn't censored very much. We could tell them what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, did you know now about concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we know just that there was-- I'm trying to remember if we had heard some of the first reports. Not really. We knew there were German camps that were very bad news, where they were very cruel to people, yes. That much we were sure of. We hadn't heard any-- as I recall, by the time we saw the first concentration camp at Landsberg, we knew it was-- that they're brutal. They're bad. But how bad, we did not quite understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance at all to read any, you know, military publications while you were there, and was there anything mentioned in these publications about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, not anything I ever saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, now, where did you go next? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we crossed the Rhine, and we kept going to Wurzburg. We were way behind the German lines. We were so far behind the German lines one night, I could look back and see the gun flashes on the main front behind us, like sheet lightning in the distance. That was the same night that our own artillery blew the radiator out of my ambulance by mistake. They were shelling us, and we just went on to Germany to get away from them. But we went on, took-- helped our division take Wurzburg and fought around the Main River near Wurzburg. And we were heading straight east. There was nothing stopping us. We were heading for Czechoslovakia. But then Eisenhower's headquarters was worried about a possible last-ditch mountain raid down in the Bavarian Alps. They thought the Nazis were going to hold-- it was a fairy tale, they weren't, but they thought they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of going on and liberating Prague, we swung back west. Took Ansbach, Germany, and then started south, toward the Danube. And all this time, you must picture mobs of German prisoners, mobs of slave workers, liberate in every town. The-- you see, the Germans used slaves, don't forget. They reintroduced human slavery into Europe, on a large scale. Who were the slaves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poles, Czechs. Political prisoners. French. Italians. Anybody from any country. They'd just pick them up by truckloads. Greek. All the country of the Balkan. They'd pick up young men, put them in trucks-- or any age men, and women-- and use them for slave labor in Germany. Every-- The first thing we saw when we crossed the border in the Sauer Basin operation, as I recall, was Yugoslavs. They just trucked them off like human cattle and used them as slaves. The farms' workers were all non-German slave workers. The factories were staffed with slave workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, they came out yelling and screaming hysterically when they saw us coming in. And then they-- of course, they'd turn and start looting the German warehouses, and the Germans would come to us with great indignation. What-- how can you allow this to happen? We said, you brought them here, pal. It's your problem. You know? You've been starving them for years. So the rioting went on in all the towns as we went through. Because they're-- half the population of Europe had been enslaved by the Germans, I suppose. And so you continued--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we kept going south. And we came to-- the first concentration camp was on the Lech River near Landsberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what-- now when is this? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to look at the date. It's on my division [INAUDIBLE] in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg. The only name-- it was the name of the city there. Landsberg, Germany. Middle-sized city where Adolf Hitler had been held as a prisoner in the fortress after his Putsch of 1922, or '23, whenever that was. [GERMAN], imprisonment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you remember about the very first thing that you saw upon entrance to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the very first thing I saw was our-- a very good, young, assault gun commander named Lieberman, a Jewish boy from New York, getting sick in my aid station. Because he went first. I was busy with a bunch of wounded, or organizing ambulances or something. And he said, there's one of those goddamn camps up there, somebody said. And he drove up and he came back white as [INAUDIBLE], as a sheet and threw up. I gave him some morphine and brandy. He was almost hysterical. And this was a brave man, who'd been through a lot of fighting. Been battlefield promoted from a Sergeant to a lieutenant.

words.text: the very first thing I saw

speaker: interviewer
text: So the signal officer and I, my friend Sobrowski, loaded a Jeep with loaves of bread from the kitchen truck, the mess truck, and drove up toward this camp. It was in the morning. A gray, cloudy morning-- I'll never forget this-- and we're driving through these pine woods in northern Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the road was kind of muddy-- half-mud, half-rain. And we started seeing bodies lying by the road. And I-- actually, they're just all over-- any old place by the road. They were obviously going to get run over or something. I got out to just lift one, move it off to the side of the road. Seemed kind of obscene to let dead bodies lie around. And I couldn't believe-- he had one of these blue and striped-- white, striped outfits on-- prison outfits. Face-down in the mud. And I turned-- I remember lifting the body, and it was like a child's body. It was a man, a full-grown man. But I don't suppose he weighed 50 or 60 pounds. And that face came up out of the mud with the mud dribbling of it, and-- I'll never-- I can see it as if it were yesterday. The-- your eyes, you know, have fat around them, so that when you get hit with something, you don't-- it's a cushion of fat. The fat was gone, and the eyes were just in this bone sockets-- huge, like the eyes of a bird. His face was thin, like a razor. The nose was thin like a razor, and the face was just like a skull with brown paint on it. [INAUDIBLE] The lips were peeled back from the teeth in a snarl, like that, from starvation. Looked like a scream, just a silent-- I remember thinking, that man's screaming but I can't hear it. And I lifted him up and put on the side. And as we went along, we kept moving the bodies off. Later, we found out what had happened, when their-- our scout cars, which are the advance of a-- of an armored division, [INAUDIBLE] had come up. The-- these men were crawling out through the mud on their hands and knees, kissing the gas cans on scout cars and fell dead. At least they knew they'd been liberated, whatever good that did them. So we came around the corner, and then we heard a few shots. And we thought, uh-oh. Our [INAUDIBLE] carbine, because there were always SS snipers hiding in the woods and things. And we came around the corner, and there was the camp. Big barbed wire. And they had a mob of people, and an American officer standing there, shooting his pistol in the air. And the people were-- it looked bizarre. They were falling, they were lying, they were hanging onto each other, they were so weak, that-- some could stand, some were lying in the mud. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It kind of resolved itself as we came up, this inchoate form, into a lot of people. And they saw us come in with that Jeep full of loaves of bread. Well, it turned out the officer was from the American Allied military government. And his [LAUGHS] idea was he was getting their attention by shooting his pistol in the air, while he tried to get their names and addresses on a piece of paper on a clipboard, which is about the mentality of the Allied military government at that stage. [LAUGHS] Of all the useless procedures I could imagine, that was the last one. But there he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were trying to cooperate, poor people, because he was an American. They loved him. And they were falling in the mud, and he's saying, what's your name? Where do you live? [LAUGHS] They saw that food, and they-- forget the military government. They came for us, hundreds of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those old army Jeeps are kind of low to the ground. And I remember looking up at this gray, April sky, and it just disappeared in a forest of claws. I mean claws, like a Hitchcock movie coming at you. And they could not control themselves. I remember thinking, they can't-- like a stone falling. They can't help themselves. And the screaming was a very high-pitched quality, like sea birds. Later-- it was from the starvation. The nasal tissues were wasted, the pharynxes were wasted, and it came out with a high-pitched, ahh! Nasal screams. And I thought, oh my god. These people are out of control, they'll tear us to pieces. But [INAUDIBLE] the spooky thing, the sensation I can remember as I sit here this afternoon-- they clutched, and the hardest clutch of hundreds of these people-- the hardest, frenzied clutch they could make felt like feathers brushing your face. Like cotton-wool, I remember thinking. It was ghostly, like a bunch of ghosts running their hands over you. And they were clutching as hard as they could clutch. They could not re-- stop themselves when they saw that food. This went on. Well, there was a little Russian boy, a teenager, who was not as starved as the rest. He helped us get things kind of organized. But it was hard. The poor people could not control themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we kept tearing the loaves of bread in half and giving one to each person. They kept cramming it in their mouths, crying and crying. It took a long time to get things in some kind of-- but they were used to standing in lines, and order, and so on. So we saw that everybody got some bread in that particular-- until ours was used up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was standing, talking to some of them. They all spoke some German, and-- even though they were Poles and Russians and everything else. They spoke enough German to get along by that time. And I was talking to this one young Jew, and he kept talking to me between mouthfuls of bread, and he kept stroking me, saying, freunde, freunde, freunde. Friends, friends, friends. He couldn't believe we-- there we were, we were friends. And then he kept saying, [GERMAN]. Mother, father, brother, sisters-- all burned, burned, burned. And then he tried to cry. These people had no tear ducts. They couldn't make tears. [INAUDIBLE] His chest would heave in these great sobs. [SIGHS] [GERMAN] Burned, burned. That's all he could get out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we went out to the rest of the camp and took the pictures that I'll show you later. And I was almost out of film, but I was lucky because the signal officer had lots of film, of course. And he had a Sergeant Lieberman, from New York-- Liebschitz, I think his name. Liebschitz. He was with us. They took some great pictures. That's how-- we pooled pictures later on. We walked through the camp, and there were just dead bodies-- as you'll see, that you had to-- it was hard not to step on them, there so many dead bodies lying on the ground. They were just thick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an odor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the odor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first-- it was an odor we knew pretty well, and that is when something is burned and rained on. The first time you shell a town and move in, a lot of carca-- dead people, and dead animals, and they get burned and rained on. That was the old battlefield smell, the smell of something burned and dead and rained on. Dead flesh. And we walked through the camp. We saw-- we were puzzled at first. The-- we saw these remnants of the A-frame. They had A-frame huts that were about six feet high. Earth floor. And there were bodies piled up. A lot of them were burned or charred. And in some places they're just a frame of the hut left-- nothing left. And wherever the huts-- these burned huts-- had been, there were piles of blackened bodies. Contorted. And we found out what had happened from the-- Seemed that a detachment of German engineers from the TODT organization-- T-O-D-T, the Todt group-- that's the German word for death, appropriately. But the man named Todt, who was the head of the engineer group, had been-- told them to kill as many as they could before the Americans liberated the place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why, God knows. I mean, it wasn't-- the excuse you hear from the Holocaust deniers was that they had to burn people for sanitary reasons or something. But there was no sanitation. There was no anything. They could hear the American tank guns we had down the road, the American machine guns, and they went on an orgy of killing. They couldn't serve their purpose or any other purpose, except killing for the sake of killing. So they locked these people up in these A-frame huts, and they threw gasoline on, and they set fire to it, and they burned them. They just roasted them alive in these huts. And that's the bodies that we were seeing, which you'll see the pictures of. And the guards had stood around with bayonets. And of course the ground was wet, and these poor people were frenzied in their attempt to crawl out. And some of them dug their way out, and they just pinned them to the ground with bayonets through the neck, or through the heart. So-- and what purpose they thought they were serving, God only knows. But this is all over the camp. So they killed a lot of people. Out of 8,000 people, I think they killed all except three.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape now. We'll continue with the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Let me get up and cough--
